## System Modules

### Module 1: Registration & Authentication

The Registration & Authentication module serves as the entry point for both Contributors and NPO Centres to register and authenticate themselves within the system. This module includes user-friendly registration screens where users can input their contact information, type (business or personal), and other relevant details. It also manages user authentication, ensuring secure access to the system through login credentials. The module will implement password hashing for security and may utilize OAuth for third-party authentication. Upon successful registration or login, users will receive a confirmation message, and their session will be initiated, allowing access to the NPO Portal or Contributors Portal. This module is crucial for maintaining the integrity and security of user data and ensuring that only authorized users can access sensitive functionalities.

Inputs: User's contact name, type (business/personal), phone number, address, pickup time, food type, kosher status, and website URL for registration; username and password for authentication.
Outputs: Confirmation of successful registration or authentication, session token for user access, and error messages for failed attempts.

---

### Module 2: Orders Management Screen

The Orders Management Screen module is designed for NPO staff to efficiently manage daily orders from Contributors. This module provides a comprehensive interface that displays all orders, allowing staff to drill down into individual contributions and view detailed line items. Users can approve or reject orders with a simple click of a button, and the system will update the order status accordingly. Additionally, a print functionality is included to generate stickers for approved orders, which can be used for packaging. The module integrates with the database to fetch real-time order data and ensures that all changes are reflected immediately. This module is essential for maintaining an organized workflow and ensuring that all contributions are processed accurately and efficiently.

Inputs: Request for daily orders, including filters for date and status; user actions for approving or rejecting orders; request for print functionality.
Outputs: List of daily orders with detailed contributions, updated order statuses, and printed order stickers.

---

### Module 3: Reports Module

The Reports Module is a powerful tool for generating various reports related to contributions and orders. This module allows users to view daily contributions, historical orders filtered by date range or contributor, and identify weekly champions based on contribution metrics. The module will utilize complex queries to aggregate data from the database, providing insightful analytics that can help improve operational efficiency. Users can specify parameters such as date ranges or contributor IDs to customize their reports. The output will be presented in a user-friendly format, such as tables or charts, and can be exported for further analysis. This module is crucial for tracking performance, identifying trends, and making informed decisions based on data-driven insights.

Inputs: Parameters for report generation, including date range, contributor ID, and report type (daily contributions, historical orders, weekly champions).
Outputs: Generated reports in tabular or graphical format, including data summaries and insights based on the specified parameters.

---

### Module 4: Orders Screen (For Contributors)

The Orders Screen module for Contributors provides a user-friendly interface for managing their contributions and orders. Contributors can view a list of their previous orders, which includes details such as order ID, date, and status. This module also allows users to create new orders by specifying a target date and adding line items, including item descriptions, quantities, and time made. Contributors can edit or delete line items as needed before submitting the order. Additionally, the module includes functionality to generate a QR code from the Centre's website URL, which can be printed for easy access. This module is essential for Contributors to track their contributions and ensure they meet the requirements for their orders.

Inputs: Request to view previous orders; details for creating a new order, including target date, item descriptions, quantities, and time made; request to generate QR code.
Outputs: List of previous orders, confirmation of new order creation, and generated QR code for the Centre's website.

---

### Module 5: GoogleMaps Service

The GoogleMaps Service module is responsible for generating a daily route for Contributors to deliver their food contributions efficiently. This module will integrate with the Google Maps API to calculate optimal routes based on the addresses of the Centres and the Contributors' pickup locations. Users will input their starting point and destination, and the module will return a Google Maps URL that displays the recommended route. This service is essential for ensuring timely deliveries and minimizing travel time, which is crucial for maintaining the quality of food contributions. The module will also consider factors such as traffic conditions and distance to provide the best possible route. This integration enhances the overall user experience by simplifying the logistics of food delivery.

Inputs: Starting point (Contributor's address) and destination (Centre's address) for route generation.
Outputs: Generated Google Maps URL with the optimal route for food delivery.

---

### Module 6: AI Service

The AI Service module is designed to enhance the system's ability to assess the quality of food contributions based on specific criteria. This module will analyze the time made and item descriptions of food items to determine if they are still suitable for donation. By utilizing machine learning algorithms, the AI can predict the freshness of food items and flag those that may be too old or unsuitable. This service will run in the background and provide recommendations to the Orders Management Screen, ensuring that only high-quality contributions are accepted. The AI Service is crucial for maintaining food safety standards and ensuring that the contributions meet the necessary quality requirements. This module will also learn from past data to improve its accuracy over time, making it an invaluable asset to the overall system.

Inputs: Data regarding food contributions, including time made and item descriptions for analysis.
Outputs: Recommendations on the suitability of food items for donation, including flags for items that may be too old.

---

## Data Model

### Data Model 1: Database Schema

CREATE TABLE Contributors (
    contributor_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    contact_name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    email VARCHAR(255),
    type ENUM('business', 'personal') NOT NULL,
    phone VARCHAR(15),
    address TEXT,
    pickup_time TIME,
    food_type VARCHAR(255),
    kosher BOOLEAN,
    website_url VARCHAR(255),
    CONSTRAINT chk_contributor_type CHECK (type IN ('business', 'personal'))
);

CREATE TABLE Orders (
    order_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    contributor_id INT NOT NULL,
    order_identifier VARCHAR(255) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    date DATE,
    day_of_week ENUM('Sunday', 'Monday', 'Tuesday', 'Wednesday', 'Thursday', 'Friday', 'Saturday'),
    target_centre VARCHAR(255) DEFAULT 'Gagon1',
    status ENUM('new', 'approved', 'rejected') DEFAULT 'new',
    google_maps_url VARCHAR(255),
    FOREIGN KEY (contributor_id) REFERENCES Contributors(contributor_id),
    CONSTRAINT chk_order_status CHECK (status IN ('new', 'approved', 'rejected')),
    CONSTRAINT chk_target_centre CHECK (
        target_centre = 'Gagon1' OR 
        (type = 'personal' OR day_of_week IN ('Friday', 'Saturday') OR NOT kosher)
    )
);

CREATE TABLE Contributions (
    contribution_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    order_id INT NOT NULL,
    item_description TEXT NOT NULL,
    quantity INT NOT NULL CHECK (quantity > 0),
    time_made TIME NOT NULL,
    recommended BOOLEAN,
    FOREIGN KEY (order_id) REFERENCES Orders(order_id)
);

CREATE TABLE Centres (
    centre_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    contact_person VARCHAR(255),
    phone VARCHAR(15),
    email VARCHAR(255),
    address TEXT NOT NULL,
    type VARCHAR(255),
    website_url VARCHAR(255)
);

CREATE INDEX idx_contributor_contact_name ON Contributors(contact_name);
CREATE INDEX idx_order_contributor_id ON Orders(contributor_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_contribution_order_id ON Contributions(order_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_centre_address ON Centres(address);

SystemDesignFormatted:
**System Design Summary**

1. **System Type & Architecture Pattern**
   - The system is a web-based application designed to manage food contributions and orders between contributors and non-profit organizations (NPOs).
   - It follows a microservices architecture pattern, allowing for modular development and scalability.

2. **Key Technologies Recommended**
   - Frontend: React.js or Angular for a dynamic user interface.
   - Backend: Node.js with Express for RESTful API development.
   - Database: PostgreSQL for relational data management.
   - AI Service: Python with machine learning libraries for food freshness determination.
   - Google Maps API for route generation.
   - Email service: SendGrid or similar for push notifications.

3. **Main Components & Their Purpose**
   - **Module 1: Registration & Authentication** - Manages user registration and authentication for contributors and NPOs.
   - **Module 2: Orders Management** - Allows NPOs to view, approve, or reject orders and print labels for contributions.
   - **Module 3: Reports** - Provides insights into daily contributions and historical order data for analysis.
   - **Module 4: Orders Screen for Contributors** - Enables contributors to view past orders and create new contributions.
   - **Module 5: Google Maps Service** - Generates daily routes for food pickups.
   - **Module 6: AI Service** - Assesses food freshness based on time made and item description.
   - **Module 7: PushEmail Service** - Sends notifications to contributors regarding their orders and reminders.

4. **Database & Data Strategy**
   - The database will consist of four main tables: Contributors, Orders, Contributions, and Centres.
   - Relationships will be established using foreign keys to ensure data integrity.
   - Regular backups and data archiving strategies will be implemented to maintain data availability and security.

5. **Deployment & Infrastructure**
   - The application will be deployed on a cloud platform such as AWS or Azure for scalability and reliability.
   - Docker containers will be used for microservices to ensure consistent environments across development and production.
   - A CI/CD pipeline will be established for automated testing and deployment, ensuring rapid iteration and updates.

This design provides a comprehensive framework for developing a robust system that meets the needs of contributors and NPOs while ensuring scalability and maintainability.

CustomerPastStory:
The Whiteboard Chronicles: Life Before the App
It’s 2:00 PM on a Tuesday, and Sarah is currently holding a desk phone to her ear with her shoulder while frantically scribbling on a sticky note. On the other end of the line is a restaurant manager screaming over the sound of a professional dishwasher.

"I’ve got six trays of pasta and four of roasted veg! Can you pick it up?" the manager yells. Sarah glances at her whiteboard. She has to manually check: Is this restaurant a "Regular"? What is their address? Do they keep Kosher?

The Logic Puzzle
If the donor is a private individual or if the food isn't Kosher, Sarah has a "Golden Rule" tattooed in her brain: It must go to Gagon1. Why? Because Gagon1 is the only facility equipped to sort uncertified or small-batch donations. Today is Tuesday, but if it were Friday night or Saturday, everything would go to Gagon1 regardless, because the other centers are closed or shorthanded. Currently, Sarah makes these routing decisions manually. Sometimes she forgets, sending a non-Kosher delivery to a strictly Kosher center, resulting in high-tension phone calls and wasted food.

The Safety Guesswork
By 4:00 PM, Sarah has a pile of fifteen sticky notes. One says: "Beef Stew - Made at 10:00 AM." Sarah bites her lip. Is it still safe to distribute by the time the driver gets there at 6:00 PM? She doesn't have a guide. She relies on her "gut feeling" and a dusty manual on food safety. She dreams of a system that could just tell her if the "Time Made" versus the "Item Description" puts the food at risk.

Logistics Chaos
Then comes the "Map Hour." Sarah opens a standard Google Maps tab and starts typing in twelve different restaurant addresses. She tries to drag the little blue line to create a logical path for David, their only volunteer driver. It takes her forty-five minutes to plan a route that David will inevitably ignore because he’ll lose the piece of paper Sarah wrote the directions on.

The Labeling Nightmare
When the food finally arrives at the NPO, the boxes are a mess. Some have "Chicken" written in Sharpie on a piece of masking tape; others have nothing at all. Sarah spends her evenings hand-writing labels so the distribution centers know what’s inside. If a center wants to know more about the donor, they have to call Sarah, who then has to dig through a physical filing cabinet to find a phone number or a website URL.

The Forgotten Champions
At the end of the month, the Board of Directors asks, "Who were our top contributors?" Sarah groans. She has to spend six hours with a calculator and a stack of greasy paper logs to figure out who gave the most. Even worse, she realizes that "The Pizza Place," which used to give every Monday, hasn't called in three weeks. She was so busy she didn't even notice they had stopped contributing.

Sarah hangs up the phone, wipes a smudge of highlighter off her forehead, and sighs. "There has to be a better way to track all this."
ShortBrief: FoodRoute is a digital initiative that bridges urban food surplus with communities in need, delivering essential support with dignity while empowering children and youth in underserved areas across diverse communities. Providing approximately 2,000 hot meals daily, the program rescues high-quality surplus from bakeries, suppliers, restaurants, hotels, and corporate kitchens. To scale impact and reduce waste, the platform transforms manual coordination into a real-time ecosystem. Through frictionless donor reporting, AI-enhanced quality assessment, and synchronized logistics management, FoodRoute streamlines contributions, safeguards food standards, improves transparency, strengthens partnerships, and ensures rescued meals efficiently reach those who depend on them.
Mission: To turn excess food into meals for those in need, using smart coordination to reduce waste and deliver impact at scale.

Title: MatchAble
ExtendedDescription:
Seeker Module
The platform provides a web application designed for job seekers with disabilities who do not hold formal professional certifications. Many individuals in this population face barriers when entering the workforce because traditional hiring processes rely heavily on resumes and formal qualifications. The system addresses this challenge by allowing users to build a structured employment profile focused on practical abilities rather than credentials. Through guided questions and structured inputs, users describe their capabilities, work preferences, physical or cognitive limitations, and the environments in which they are most likely to succeed. The profile gradually captures meaningful information about the individual’s functional strengths, enabling the system to represent each candidate in a more realistic and inclusive way.

Employer Module
On the employer side, the platform provides a web interface that allows organizations to register their businesses and define job opportunities that may be suitable for candidates with disabilities. Employers can describe the responsibilities of each role, the tasks involved, the work environment, required skills, and accessibility considerations. Instead of requiring traditional job descriptions centered on formal qualifications, the system encourages employers to describe positions in terms of tasks and workplace conditions. This approach enables a more flexible and inclusive definition of roles while helping employers identify candidates whose practical abilities align with the real requirements of the job.

Matching Module
At the center of the platform is an AI-based matching service that analyzes both job seeker profiles and job characteristics to identify the most suitable employment opportunities. The AI evaluates multiple parameters, including functional abilities, task requirements, environmental conditions, accessibility needs, and user preferences. Using these inputs, the service generates compatibility scores between candidates and available job opportunities. Job seekers receive a ranked list of opportunities that best match their capabilities, while employers can discover candidates who demonstrate strong compatibility with the roles they define. This AI-driven approach allows the system to continuously improve its recommendations as more profiles and job descriptions are added to the platform.

Reporting Module
The platform operates through a nonprofit middle tier responsible for managing participation and ensuring alignment with the broader mission of inclusive employment. To support this role, the system includes a reporting and analytics module designed specifically for nonprofit operators. This module provides aggregated insights into platform activity, including candidate registrations, job postings, matching activity, and employment outcomes. The reporting system enables the nonprofit organization to monitor engagement levels, track placement trends, and identify sectors where additional job opportunities are needed. These insights help guide partnerships with employers, training providers, and community organizations while supporting data-driven improvements to the program.

Accessibility API Module
To support users who may have difficulty interacting with traditional text-based interfaces, the platform integrates a backend accessibility service using Google Cloud Speech-to-Text. This service allows users to respond to profile questions through voice recordings rather than typing. Audio input is securely transmitted to the backend infrastructure, where the speech recognition service converts spoken responses into structured text. The processed data is then incorporated into the user’s employment profile within the system. By handling speech processing entirely through backend services, the platform enables voice-based interaction while keeping the client interface simple and accessible for users with different accessibility needs.
SystemDesign:
# System Design for MatchAble

**Project Description:** A digital employment platform connects job seekers with disabilities who lack formal certifications with inclusive job opportunities. Through a web application, users create structured profiles describing their abilities, preferences, limitations, and preferred work environments, focusing on practical capabilities rather than formal credentials. Employers use a web interface to register their organizations and define roles adapted for candidates with disabilities, including workplace conditions and accessibility requirements. An AI-based matching service analyzes both profiles and job characteristics to recommend suitable opportunities. The platform is operated through a nonprofit middle tier with reporting tools for monitoring activity and outcomes, and integrates Google Cloud Speech-to-Text to enable voice-based user input.

**Extended Description:** Seeker Module
The platform provides a web application designed for job seekers with disabilities who do not hold formal professional certifications. Many individuals in this population face barriers when entering the workforce because traditional hiring processes rely heavily on resumes and formal qualifications. The system addresses this challenge by allowing users to build a structured employment profile focused on practical abilities rather than credentials. Through guided questions and structured inputs, users describe their capabilities, work preferences, physical or cognitive limitations, and the environments in which they are most likely to succeed. The profile gradually captures meaningful information about the individual’s functional strengths, enabling the system to represent each candidate in a more realistic and inclusive way.

Employer Module
On the employer side, the platform provides a web interface that allows organizations to register their businesses and define job opportunities that may be suitable for candidates with disabilities. Employers can describe the responsibilities of each role, the tasks involved, the work environment, required skills, and accessibility considerations. Instead of requiring traditional job descriptions centered on formal qualifications, the system encourages employers to describe positions in terms of tasks and workplace conditions. This approach enables a more flexible and inclusive definition of roles while helping employers identify candidates whose practical abilities align with the real requirements of the job.

Matching Module
At the center of the platform is an AI-based matching service that analyzes both job seeker profiles and job characteristics to identify the most suitable employment opportunities. The AI evaluates multiple parameters, including functional abilities, task requirements, environmental conditions, accessibility needs, and user preferences. Using these inputs, the service generates compatibility scores between candidates and available job opportunities. Job seekers receive a ranked list of opportunities that best match their capabilities, while employers can discover candidates who demonstrate strong compatibility with the roles they define. This AI-driven approach allows the system to continuously improve its recommendations as more profiles and job descriptions are added to the platform.

Reporting Module
The platform operates through a nonprofit middle tier responsible for managing participation and ensuring alignment with the broader mission of inclusive employment. To support this role, the system includes a reporting and analytics module designed specifically for nonprofit operators. This module provides aggregated insights into platform activity, including candidate registrations, job postings, matching activity, and employment outcomes. The reporting system enables the nonprofit organization to monitor engagement levels, track placement trends, and identify sectors where additional job opportunities are needed. These insights help guide partnerships with employers, training providers, and community organizations while supporting data-driven improvements to the program.

Accessibility API Module
To support users who may have difficulty interacting with traditional text-based interfaces, the platform integrates a backend accessibility service using Google Cloud Speech-to-Text. This service allows users to respond to profile questions through voice recordings rather than typing. Audio input is securely transmitted to the backend infrastructure, where the speech recognition service converts spoken responses into structured text. The processed data is then incorporated into the user’s employment profile within the system. By handling speech processing entirely through backend services, the platform enables voice-based interaction while keeping the client interface simple and accessible for users with different accessibility needs.

---

## System Modules

### Module 1: Seeker Profile Module

The Seeker Profile Module is designed to facilitate the creation and management of employment profiles for job seekers with disabilities. This module guides users through a series of structured questions that help them articulate their practical abilities, work preferences, and any physical or cognitive limitations they may have. The module captures detailed information about the user's strengths and the environments in which they thrive. It also allows users to update their profiles as their skills and preferences evolve. The data collected is crucial for matching candidates with suitable job opportunities, ensuring a more inclusive hiring process.

Inputs: User responses to guided questions about abilities, preferences, limitations, and work environments.
Outputs: A structured employment profile that includes the user's capabilities, preferences, and limitations.

---

### Module 2: Employer Registration Module

The Employer Registration Module serves as the entry point for organizations looking to post job opportunities tailored for candidates with disabilities. This module allows employers to create an account, providing essential business information such as company name, contact details, and a description of their mission regarding inclusive hiring. Employers can then define job roles by outlining responsibilities, required skills, and accessibility considerations. The module emphasizes a flexible approach to job descriptions, encouraging employers to focus on tasks and workplace conditions rather than formal qualifications. This fosters a more inclusive environment for job seekers.

Inputs: Business information from employers, including company name, contact details, and job role descriptions.
Outputs: A registered employer profile that includes job postings and organizational details.

---

### Module 3: Job Posting Module

The Job Posting Module allows registered employers to create and manage job listings specifically designed for candidates with disabilities. Employers can input detailed descriptions of job responsibilities, required skills, and the work environment, focusing on practical tasks rather than formal qualifications. This module also includes features for editing and updating job postings as needed. By streamlining the job posting process, this module ensures that opportunities are clearly communicated to potential candidates. Additionally, it integrates with the matching module to facilitate the identification of suitable candidates based on the job characteristics defined by the employer.

Inputs: Job details from employers, including responsibilities, required skills, and environmental conditions.
Outputs: A published job listing that is accessible to job seekers and integrated into the matching system.

---

### Module 4: AI Matching Module

The AI Matching Module is the core component of the platform, utilizing advanced algorithms to analyze both job seeker profiles and job postings to generate compatibility scores. This module evaluates a variety of parameters, including functional abilities, task requirements, environmental conditions, and user preferences. By employing machine learning techniques, the module continuously improves its matching accuracy as more data is collected. Job seekers receive a ranked list of job opportunities that align with their capabilities, while employers are presented with candidates who best fit their defined roles. This dynamic matching process enhances the overall employment experience for both parties.

Inputs: Data from job seeker profiles and job postings, including abilities, preferences, and job characteristics.
Outputs: A ranked list of job opportunities for seekers and a list of compatible candidates for employers.

---

### Module 5: Reporting and Analytics Module

The Reporting and Analytics Module is designed for nonprofit operators to monitor and evaluate the platform's performance and impact on inclusive employment. This module aggregates data on candidate registrations, job postings, matching activities, and employment outcomes. By providing visual analytics and reports, it enables stakeholders to track engagement levels, identify trends in job placements, and recognize sectors that require more job opportunities. The insights generated from this module are essential for guiding partnerships with employers, training providers, and community organizations, ensuring that the platform aligns with its mission of promoting inclusive employment practices.

Inputs: Data on platform activities, including registrations, job postings, and matching statistics.
Outputs: Aggregated reports and analytics that provide insights into platform performance and engagement.

---

### Module 6: Accessibility API Module

The Accessibility API Module integrates a backend service that enhances user interaction for those who may struggle with traditional text-based interfaces. Utilizing Google Cloud Speech-to-Text technology, this module allows users to respond to profile questions using voice recordings. The audio input is securely transmitted to the backend, where it is processed and converted into structured text. This text is then incorporated into the user's employment profile, ensuring that users with different accessibility needs can effectively engage with the platform. By focusing on voice-based interaction, this module significantly improves the user experience for individuals with disabilities.

Inputs: Voice recordings from users responding to profile questions.
Outputs: Structured text data that is integrated into the user's employment profile.

---

## Data Model

### Data Model 1: Database Schema

CREATE TABLE Users (
    user_id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    username VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    email VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    password_hash VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    updated_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ON UPDATE CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    -- Stores basic user information
    -- user_id: Primary key for user identification
    -- username: Unique username for login
    -- email: Unique email for contact
    -- password_hash: Securely stored password
    -- created_at: Timestamp of account creation
    -- updated_at: Timestamp of last update
    CONSTRAINT chk_email_format CHECK (email LIKE '%_@__%.__%')
);

CREATE TABLE SeekerProfiles (
    profile_id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    user_id INT NOT NULL,
    abilities TEXT,
    preferences TEXT,
    limitations TEXT,
    work_environments TEXT,
    updated_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ON UPDATE CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES Users(user_id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    -- Stores detailed employment profiles for job seekers
    -- profile_id: Primary key for profile identification
    -- user_id: Foreign key linking to Users table
    -- abilities: Text describing user's abilities
    -- preferences: Text describing user's work preferences
    -- limitations: Text describing user's limitations
    -- work_environments: Text describing suitable work environments
    -- updated_at: Timestamp of last profile update
);

CREATE TABLE Employers (
    employer_id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    company_name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    contact_email VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    contact_phone VARCHAR(20),
    mission_statement TEXT,
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    updated_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ON UPDATE CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    -- Stores employer information
    -- employer_id: Primary key for employer identification
    -- company_name: Name of the company
    -- contact_email: Email for employer contact
    -- contact_phone: Phone number for employer contact
    -- mission_statement: Text describing company's mission on inclusive hiring
    -- created_at: Timestamp of employer registration
    -- updated_at: Timestamp of last update
    CONSTRAINT chk_contact_email_format CHECK (contact_email LIKE '%_@__%.__%')
);

CREATE TABLE JobPostings (
    job_id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    employer_id INT NOT NULL,
    job_title VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    responsibilities TEXT,
    required_skills TEXT,
    work_environment TEXT,
    accessibility_considerations TEXT,
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    updated_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ON UPDATE CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    FOREIGN KEY (employer_id) REFERENCES Employers(employer_id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    -- Stores job postings by employers
    -- job_id: Primary key for job identification
    -- employer_id: Foreign key linking to Employers table
    -- job_title: Title of the job position
    -- responsibilities: Text describing job responsibilities
    -- required_skills: Text describing skills required for the job
    -- work_environment: Text describing the work environment
    -- accessibility_considerations: Text describing accessibility considerations
    -- created_at: Timestamp of job posting creation
    -- updated_at: Timestamp of last update
);

CREATE TABLE Matches (
    match_id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    seeker_profile_id INT NOT NULL,
    job_id INT NOT NULL,
    compatibility_score DECIMAL(5, 2) NOT NULL,
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    FOREIGN KEY (seeker_profile_id) REFERENCES SeekerProfiles(profile_id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    FOREIGN KEY (job_id) REFERENCES JobPostings(job_id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    -- Stores matches between job seekers and job postings
    -- match_id: Primary key for match identification
    -- seeker_profile_id: Foreign key linking to SeekerProfiles table
    -- job_id: Foreign key linking to JobPostings table
    -- compatibility_score: Numeric score indicating compatibility
    -- created_at: Timestamp of match creation
);

CREATE TABLE Reports (
    report_id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    report_type VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    generated_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    data JSON,
    -- Stores generated reports and analytics data
    -- report_id: Primary key for report identification
    -- report_type: Type of report (e.g., registration, job posting)
    -- generated_at: Timestamp of report generation
    -- data: JSON object containing report data
);

CREATE TABLE VoiceInputs (
    input_id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    user_id INT NOT NULL,
    audio_data BLOB,
    processed_text TEXT,
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES Users(user_id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    -- Stores voice inputs from users
    -- input_id: Primary key for input identification
    -- user_id: Foreign key linking to Users table
    -- audio_data: Binary data of the voice recording
    -- processed_text: Text processed from the audio data
    -- created_at: Timestamp of input creation
);

CREATE INDEX idx_user_email ON Users(email);
CREATE INDEX idx_employer_email ON Employers(contact_email);
CREATE INDEX idx_job_title ON JobPostings(job_title);
CREATE INDEX idx_match_score ON Matches(compatibility_score);

SystemDesignFormatted:
**System Design Summary for Seeker Module**

1. **System Type & Architecture Pattern**
   - The system is a web-based application designed to facilitate job matching for individuals with disabilities.
   - It follows a microservices architecture, allowing for modular development and scalability.

2. **Key Technologies Recommended**
   - Frontend: React.js for a responsive user interface.
   - Backend: Node.js with Express for RESTful API services.
   - Database: MongoDB for flexible data storage.
   - AI Matching: Python with machine learning libraries (e.g., TensorFlow, Scikit-learn) for the matching algorithm.
   - Accessibility: Google Cloud Speech-to-Text for voice input processing.

3. **Main Components & Their Purpose**
   - **Seeker Module**: Allows users to create and manage their employment profiles based on practical abilities.
   - **Employer Module**: Enables employers to post job opportunities and describe roles in terms of tasks and conditions.
   - **Matching Module**: Utilizes AI to analyze profiles and job descriptions, generating compatibility scores to facilitate job matching.
   - **Reporting Module**: Provides analytics for nonprofit operators to monitor platform activity and engagement.
   - **Accessibility API Module**: Integrates voice recognition capabilities to enhance user interaction for those with accessibility needs.

4. **Database & Data Strategy**
   - MongoDB will be used to store user profiles, job postings, and matching data, allowing for dynamic schema management.
   - Data will be structured to support efficient querying and retrieval for matching and reporting purposes.
   - Regular data audits will ensure data integrity and compliance with privacy regulations.

5. **Deployment & Infrastructure**
   - The application will be deployed on a cloud platform (e.g., AWS or Google Cloud) to ensure scalability and reliability.
   - A CI/CD pipeline will be established for continuous integration and deployment, facilitating regular updates and feature enhancements.
   - Load balancers and auto-scaling groups will be configured to handle varying traffic loads efficiently.

This design aims to create an inclusive, user-friendly platform that effectively connects job seekers with disabilities to suitable employment opportunities while supporting employers in their hiring processes.

CustomerPastStory:
The Spreadsheet Chronicles: Life Before the Platform

It’s 9:00 AM on a Sunday, and Maya, the nonprofit employment coordinator, is already on her third phone call. On the line is a parent describing her son’s strengths: “He’s great with routines, loves organizing tools, and works best in quiet spaces.” Maya scribbles notes on a printed intake form. There’s no structured template—just bullet points and question marks.

The Intake Maze

Each candidate arrives with a different story. Some bring partial resumes; others bring nothing at all. Maya tries to translate conversations into something employers will understand. She manually writes summaries like “Good fine motor skills” or “Needs predictable schedule,” hoping she captured the nuance correctly.

The Employer Guesswork

Later that afternoon, a local warehouse manager calls. “We need someone to sort small parts and label boxes,” he says. Maya flips through a binder of candidate files. Who can stand for long periods? Who prefers repetitive tasks? She relies on memory and handwritten notes, afraid she’ll overlook someone suitable.

The Matching Gamble

There is no system—only instinct. Maya emails a candidate’s phone number to the employer and hopes for the best. Sometimes the placement works beautifully. Other times, misunderstandings about workload, noise levels, or supervision cause the job to fall apart within weeks.

The Reporting Headache

At the end of the quarter, the board asks for numbers: How many candidates registered? How many interviews? How many placements lasted more than three months? Maya opens three spreadsheets and a stack of folders, manually counting rows and piecing together partial data.

The Accessibility Struggle

Some candidates struggle to fill out digital forms independently. Maya often types responses for them during meetings, rephrasing spoken answers into written summaries, worried she may unintentionally distort their voice.

By 6:00 PM, Maya leans back in her chair, surrounded by folders and sticky notes. “There has to be a better way to match people’s abilities to real jobs,” she whispers.
ShortBrief: MatchAble is a digital employment platform that connects job seekers with disabilities who lack formal certifications to inclusive, meaningful job opportunities. Through a web application, users build structured profiles capturing their abilities, work preferences, limitations, and ideal environments, emphasizing practical capabilities over formal credentials. Employers register their organizations and define roles tailored for candidates with disabilities, including accessibility considerations. An AI-based matching service analyzes both job seeker profiles and role characteristics to recommend suitable opportunities. Operated via a nonprofit middle tier with reporting tools, the platform also integrates Google Cloud Speech-to-Text to support voice-based user input.
Mission: To connect people with disabilities to meaningful job opportunities by focusing on abilities over credentials, using AI-powered matching to create employment opportunities at scale.
OneLiner: Unlocking Jobs for People with Disabilities

Title: SafePath
ExtendedDescription:
Project: SafePath – Community Powered Urban Accessibility Mapping

SafePath is a community-driven digital platform designed to improve urban mobility, safety, and independence for people with disabilities. For individuals with mobility challenges, what should be a simple walk can quickly become unsafe due to broken sidewalks, blocked ramps, steep slopes, construction zones, poor lighting, or temporary obstacles. Traditional navigation applications do not account for these real-world accessibility barriers. SafePath closes this gap by combining structured citizen reporting, intelligent route planning, municipal management tools, automated analytics, and AI-based prioritization into one integrated system.

The platform is built around five core modules.

1. Mobile Accessibility Hazard Reporting Interface
At the heart of SafePath is a structured mobile reporting form designed to transform citizen input into actionable municipal data. Each report includes predefined fields to ensure consistency and automation readiness. Users submit:

Hazard type (broken pavement, missing ramp, obstruction, slope, surface issue, lighting issue, construction zone)

Automatic GPS location

Photo upload

Affected disability group (mobility, visual, hearing, cognitive, elderly)

Obstruction level (partial / full blockage)

User-rated severity (1–5)

Optional short description

Time and date stamp (automatic)

Because the data is structured at the point of entry, it eliminates ambiguity and enables automatic aggregation, filtering, and reporting without manual interpretation.

2. Municipal Operations Dashboard
A centralized dashboard provides city officials with a real-time overview of all reported hazards. Reports can be filtered and sorted by hazard type, severity score, district, affected disability group, frequency of reports per location, time since submission, and resolution status. The dashboard supports task assignment, status updates, and progress tracking, allowing maintenance teams to manage workflows efficiently and transparently.

3. Accessible Route Planning Module
Built on the Google Maps API, the routing engine calculates optimized routes tailored to personal accessibility parameters such as wheelchair width, slope tolerance, surface preferences, and avoidance of high-severity zones. The engine dynamically integrates active hazard reports and AI-generated severity scores to guide users along safer paths. This effectively functions as a “Waze for accessibility.”

4. Automated Municipal Reporting Module
Based entirely on the structured data submitted by users, the system automatically generates five key reports:

Dynamic Severity Index Report

Accessibility Hotspot Density Report

Disability Impact Distribution Report

Recurring Hazard & Repeat Location Report

Response & Resolution Performance Report

These reports require no manual compilation and provide measurable operational and strategic insights for budgeting, infrastructure planning, and service improvement.

5. AI-Based Prioritization Engine
An embedded AI service enhances prioritization by calculating a dynamic severity score for each hazard. The engine considers user-rated severity, report frequency, obstruction level, proximity to critical public institutions, pedestrian density, and historical recurrence patterns. This score feeds directly into the dashboard, routing engine, and Severity Index Report, ensuring that the most impactful hazards are addressed first.

SafePath converts structured community participation into intelligent urban management, enabling municipalities to respond faster, allocate resources smarter, and build safer, more inclusive cities.

# System Design for SafePath

The system aggregates this data into an intelligent municipal dashboard, automated reports, and an AI-based severity index. Integrated with Google Maps for accessible route planning, SafePath helps cities prioritize critical issues, optimize maintenance resources, and provide safer navigation for people with disabilities.

SafePath converts structured community participation into intelligent urban management, enabling municipalities to respond faster, allocate resources smarter, and build safer, more inclusive cities.

---

## System Modules

### Module 1: Mobile Accessibility Hazard Reporting Interface

The Mobile Accessibility Hazard Reporting Interface serves as the primary tool for citizens to report accessibility hazards in urban environments. This module features a structured reporting form that includes predefined fields to ensure data consistency and facilitate automation. Users can select the hazard type, which includes options such as broken pavement or missing ramps, and provide their GPS location automatically. They can also upload photos, specify the affected disability group, and rate the severity of the hazard on a scale from 1 to 5. The module captures an optional short description and automatically timestamps each report. By standardizing the data input, this module enables efficient aggregation, filtering, and reporting, eliminating ambiguity and manual interpretation. This structured approach allows municipalities to respond quickly to reported hazards, ultimately improving urban mobility and safety for individuals with disabilities. The interface is designed to be user-friendly, ensuring that citizens can easily navigate the reporting process and contribute valuable information to the community.

Inputs: Hazard type (broken pavement, missing ramp, obstruction, slope, surface issue, lighting issue, construction zone), Automatic GPS location, Photo upload, Affected disability group (mobility, visual, hearing, cognitive, elderly), Obstruction level (partial / full blockage), User-rated severity (1–5), Optional short description, Time and date stamp (automatic)
Outputs: Structured hazard report containing all input data, Confirmation of report submission, Automatic timestamp, Aggregated data for municipal use.

---

### Module 2: Municipal Operations Dashboard

The Municipal Operations Dashboard is a centralized interface designed for city officials to monitor and manage reported accessibility hazards in real-time. This module aggregates data from citizen reports, enabling officials to filter and sort hazards by various criteria, including type, severity score, district, and affected disability group. The dashboard features advanced visual representations of data, such as interactive maps and dynamic charts, which enhance understanding and facilitate informed decision-making.

City officials can assign tasks to maintenance teams directly through the dashboard, update the status of reported hazards, and track progress transparently. The integration with the reporting module ensures that all data is current and reflects the latest submissions from citizens. Additionally, the dashboard supports automated notifications for new reports and reminders for pending tasks, allowing city officials to prioritize their responses effectively.

This module is essential for improving operational efficiency and ensuring that resources are allocated appropriately to address the most pressing accessibility issues within the community. 

**Inputs:** 
- Real-time hazard report data from the Mobile Accessibility Hazard Reporting Interface
- Filters for hazard type, severity score, district, affected disability group, and resolution status

**Outputs:** 
- Visualized data representation of reported hazards
- Task assignment confirmations
- Status updates for hazards
- Progress tracking information
- Notifications for new reports and pending tasks

---

### Module 3: Accessible Route Planning Module

The Accessible Route Planning Module empowers SafePath users to navigate urban environments safely by avoiding reported accessibility hazards. This module accepts a source and destination as input and calculates optimal walking or rolling routes while dynamically eliminating paths that intersect known hazards.

To achieve this, the module utilizes the Google Maps Directions API with alternative route options enabled. Each reported hazard is represented as a geospatial point or small polygon around its GPS location. When multiple routes are returned from the API, the module systematically evaluates each route against the set of hazards. By decoding the route polyline into individual coordinates, the system checks for intersections with hazard polygons. Routes that intersect any hazard are discarded, ensuring that only safe alternatives are presented to the user.

This methodology allows SafePath to implement real-time hazard-aware routing without the need for complex AI or graph algorithms. It operates entirely on the structured hazard data reported by the community, making it straightforward to maintain and update as new hazards are reported. The module also visually highlights hazards on the map, enhancing user awareness and confidence in route planning.

The Accessible Route Planning Module not only enhances the independence of individuals with mobility challenges but also promotes inclusivity by ensuring that urban navigation is accessible to all.

Inputs: 
- User-defined source and destination
- Real-time hazard report data (geospatial points/polygons)

Outputs:
- Optimized walking or rolling route directions
- Turn-by-turn navigation instructions
- Alerts for hazards along the route
- Visual representation of hazards on the map
- Estimated travel time

**Inputs:** - User-defined source and destination
- Real-time hazard report data (geospatial points/polygons)

**Outputs:** - Optimized walking or rolling route directions
- Turn-by-turn navigation instructions
- Alerts for hazards along the route
- Visual representation of hazards on the map
- Estimated travel time

---

### Module 4: Automated Municipal Reporting Module

The Automated Municipal Reporting Module is designed to streamline the generation of key operational reports based on the structured data submitted by users through the hazard reporting interface. This module automatically compiles five essential reports: the Dynamic Severity Index Report, which assesses the overall severity of reported hazards; the Accessibility Hotspot Density Report, which identifies areas with high concentrations of accessibility issues; the Disability Impact Distribution Report, which analyzes the types of disabilities affected by reported hazards; the Recurring Hazard & Repeat Location Report, which tracks persistent issues over time; and the Response & Resolution Performance Report, which evaluates the efficiency of municipal responses to reported hazards. By automating this reporting process, the module eliminates the need for manual data compilation, allowing city officials to focus on strategic decision-making and resource allocation. The reports generated provide critical insights for budgeting, infrastructure planning, and service improvement, ultimately enhancing urban accessibility and safety for all citizens.

Inputs: Structured hazard report data from the Mobile Accessibility Hazard Reporting Interface, User feedback data, Historical report data
Outputs: Dynamic Severity Index Report, Accessibility Hotspot Density Report, Disability Impact Distribution Report, Recurring Hazard & Repeat Location Report, Response & Resolution Performance Report.

---

### Module 5: AI-Based Prioritization Engine

The AI Prioritization Agent is a core component of the SafePath platform designed to enhance the assessment and prioritization of user-reported accessibility hazards. This module dynamically assigns a severity score to each reported hazard by analyzing structured input data submitted through the mobile reporting form. The input data includes GPS location, hazard type, affected disability group, user-rated severity, obstruction level, frequency of reports, and optional descriptive notes, providing both quantitative and qualitative context for each accessibility issue.

Leveraging a large language model (LLM) in conjunction with rule-based heuristics, the AI evaluates each report by considering a multitude of contextual factors. These factors include proximity to critical public infrastructure such as hospitals, schools, and transit hubs; cumulative reporting frequency at the specific location; the type and severity of the hazard; and the potential impact on various disability groups. The LLM interprets descriptive notes and identifies patterns across multiple reports, refining the severity ranking and helping to highlight hazards that may be underreported or disproportionately impactful.

The module outputs a standardized severity score for each hazard, which is utilized to prioritize maintenance actions, generate municipal reports, and inform accessible route calculations. This prioritization process ensures that municipalities focus on the most urgent and high-impact issues first, providing actionable intelligence derived from community-generated data. Additionally, the AI service maintains transparency and traceability throughout the prioritization process, allowing stakeholders to understand the rationale behind prioritization decisions.

Inputs: GPS location data, Hazard type, Affected disability group, User-rated severity data, Obstruction level data, Report frequency data, Optional descriptive notes.  
Outputs: Standardized severity scores for each hazard, Prioritization recommendations for municipal maintenance actions, Municipal reports, Updated data for accessible route calculations. 

The AI Prioritization Agent continuously learns from incoming data, adapting its algorithms to improve the accuracy and relevance of its prioritization over time, thereby enhancing the overall safety and mobility of individuals with disabilities in urban environments.

---

### Module 6: User Authentication and Profile Management Module

The User Authentication and Profile Management Module is essential for ensuring secure access to the SafePath platform for both citizens and municipal officials. This module handles user registration, login, and authentication processes, allowing users to create and manage their profiles securely. It captures essential user information, including name, email, password, and any relevant accessibility needs. The module employs industry-standard encryption and security protocols to protect user data and ensure privacy. Additionally, it supports features such as password recovery and multi-factor authentication to enhance security. Users can update their profiles to reflect changes in their accessibility needs or personal information, ensuring that the data remains current and relevant. This module also integrates with the Municipal Operations Dashboard to provide city officials with access to user-generated reports and feedback, fostering a collaborative environment between citizens and municipal authorities. By prioritizing user security and data integrity, this module plays a crucial role in building trust within the community and encouraging active participation in reporting accessibility hazards.

Inputs: User registration data (name, email, password, accessibility needs), Login credentials (email, password), Profile update data
Outputs: User authentication status, User profile data, Confirmation of profile updates, Password recovery instructions.

---

## Data Model

### Data Model 1: Database Schema

CREATE TABLE Users (
    user_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    email VARCHAR(255) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    password_hash VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    accessibility_needs TEXT,
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    updated_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ON UPDATE CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    CONSTRAINT chk_email_format CHECK (email LIKE '%_@__%.__%')
);

CREATE TABLE HazardTypes (
    hazard_type_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    description TEXT
);

CREATE TABLE DisabilityGroups (
    disability_group_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    description TEXT
);

CREATE TABLE HazardReports (
    report_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    user_id INT NOT NULL,
    hazard_type_id INT NOT NULL,
    gps_location POINT NOT NULL,
    photo_url VARCHAR(255),
    affected_disability_group_id INT NOT NULL,
    obstruction_level ENUM('partial', 'full') NOT NULL,
    severity_rating INT CHECK (severity_rating BETWEEN 1 AND 5),
    short_description TEXT,
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES Users(user_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (hazard_type_id) REFERENCES HazardTypes(hazard_type_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (affected_disability_group_id) REFERENCES DisabilityGroups(disability_group_id)
);

CREATE TABLE MunicipalTasks (
    task_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    report_id INT NOT NULL,
    assigned_to INT,
    status ENUM('pending', 'in_progress', 'resolved') DEFAULT 'pending',
    updated_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ON UPDATE CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    FOREIGN KEY (report_id) REFERENCES HazardReports(report_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (assigned_to) REFERENCES Users(user_id)
);

CREATE TABLE RoutePreferences (
    preference_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    user_id INT NOT NULL,
    wheelchair_width DECIMAL(5, 2),
    slope_tolerance DECIMAL(5, 2),
    surface_preferences TEXT,
    FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES Users(user_id)
);

CREATE TABLE AISeverityScores (
    score_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    report_id INT NOT NULL,
    dynamic_severity_score DECIMAL(5, 2),
    calculated_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    FOREIGN KEY (report_id) REFERENCES HazardReports(report_id)
);

CREATE TABLE MunicipalReports (
    report_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    report_type ENUM('Dynamic Severity Index', 'Accessibility Hotspot Density', 'Disability Impact Distribution', 'Recurring Hazard & Repeat Location', 'Response & Resolution Performance') NOT NULL,
    generated_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    data JSON NOT NULL
);

CREATE INDEX idx_hazard_reports_user_id ON HazardReports(user_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_hazard_reports_hazard_type_id ON HazardReports(hazard_type_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_hazard_reports_disability_group_id ON HazardReports(affected_disability_group_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_municipal_tasks_report_id ON MunicipalTasks(report_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_ai_severity_scores_report_id ON AISeverityScores(report_id);

COMMENT ON TABLE Users IS 'Stores user information for both citizens and municipal officials.';
COMMENT ON COLUMN Users.name IS 'Name of the user.';
COMMENT ON COLUMN Users.email IS 'Unique email address for user identification.';
COMMENT ON COLUMN Users.password_hash IS 'Hashed password for secure authentication.';
COMMENT ON COLUMN Users.accessibility_needs IS 'Details of user-specific accessibility requirements.';

COMMENT ON TABLE HazardTypes IS 'Defines different types of accessibility hazards that can be reported.';
COMMENT ON COLUMN HazardTypes.name IS 'Name of the hazard type.';
COMMENT ON COLUMN HazardTypes.description IS 'Description of the hazard type.';

COMMENT ON TABLE DisabilityGroups IS 'Lists different disability groups affected by hazards.';
COMMENT ON COLUMN DisabilityGroups.name IS 'Name of the disability group.';
COMMENT ON COLUMN DisabilityGroups.description IS 'Description of the disability group.';

COMMENT ON TABLE HazardReports IS 'Stores reports of accessibility hazards submitted by users.';
COMMENT ON COLUMN HazardReports.gps_location IS 'GPS coordinates of the reported hazard.';
COMMENT ON COLUMN HazardReports.photo_url IS 'URL of the photo uploaded by the user.';
COMMENT ON COLUMN HazardReports.obstruction_level IS 'Level of obstruction caused by the hazard.';
COMMENT ON COLUMN HazardReports.severity_rating IS 'User-rated severity of the hazard on a scale of 1 to 5.';
COMMENT ON COLUMN HazardReports.short_description IS 'Optional short description provided by the user.';

COMMENT ON TABLE MunicipalTasks IS 'Tracks tasks assigned to municipal teams for resolving reported hazards.';
COMMENT ON COLUMN MunicipalTasks.status IS 'Current status of the task.';

COMMENT ON TABLE RoutePreferences IS 'Stores user preferences for accessible route planning.';
COMMENT ON COLUMN RoutePreferences.wheelchair_width IS 'Preferred wheelchair width for route planning.';
COMMENT ON COLUMN RoutePreferences.slope_tolerance IS 'Preferred slope tolerance for route planning.';
COMMENT ON COLUMN RoutePreferences.surface_preferences IS 'User preferences for surface types during route planning.';

COMMENT ON TABLE AISeverityScores IS 'Stores dynamic severity scores calculated by the AI-based prioritization engine.';
COMMENT ON COLUMN AISeverityScores.dynamic_severity_score IS 'Calculated severity score for prioritizing hazard resolution.';

COMMENT ON TABLE MunicipalReports IS 'Stores automatically generated municipal reports based on hazard data.';
COMMENT ON COLUMN MunicipalReports.report_type IS 'Type of report generated.';
COMMENT ON COLUMN MunicipalReports.data IS 'JSON data containing report details.';

SystemDesignFormatted:
**System Design Summary for SafePath**

1. **System Type & Architecture Pattern**
   - SafePath is a web-based platform utilizing a microservices architecture to ensure scalability and maintainability. Each module operates independently, allowing for seamless updates and integration.

2. **Key Technologies Recommended**
   - Frontend: React.js for a responsive user interface.
   - Backend: Node.js with Express for RESTful API development.
   - Database: PostgreSQL for structured data storage.
   - Cloud Services: AWS or Azure for hosting and scalability.
   - AI/ML: TensorFlow or PyTorch for the prioritization engine.

3. **Main Components & Their Purpose**
   - **Mobile Accessibility Hazard Reporting Interface**: Allows users to report hazards with structured data, ensuring clarity and consistency.
   - **Municipal Operations Dashboard**: Provides city officials with real-time insights into reported hazards, facilitating efficient management and response.
   - **Accessible Route Planning Module**: Utilizes Google Maps API to generate optimized routes based on user-defined accessibility parameters.
   - **Automated Municipal Reporting Module**: Generates key reports automatically from user-submitted data, aiding in strategic planning and resource allocation.
   - **AI-Based Prioritization Engine**: Calculates dynamic severity scores for hazards, ensuring that the most critical issues are prioritized for resolution.

4. **Database & Data Strategy**
   - PostgreSQL will be used to store structured reports, user data, and hazard information. The data strategy focuses on ensuring data integrity and enabling automated reporting through well-defined schemas.

5. **Deployment & Infrastructure**
   - The application will be deployed on a cloud platform (AWS or Azure) to ensure high availability and scalability. Containerization with Docker will be employed for microservices, allowing for easy deployment and management of individual components.

SafePath aims to transform urban accessibility management through community engagement and intelligent data utilization, fostering safer and more inclusive cities.

The Clipboard Chronicles: Life Before SafePath
It’s 2:00 PM on a Tuesday, and Alex is balancing a clipboard in one hand while typing addresses into a GPS app with the other. On the phone is a concerned citizen describing an accessibility issue near Main Street.

The Information Puzzle
Alex glances at a stack of printed forms pinned to the wall. He has to manually check: Has this issue been reported before? What is the exact location? Which disability type is most affected?

The Priority Guesswork
If the report involves a wheelchair user on a steep incline, Alex has a mental “Golden Rule”: it must be flagged as urgent. Otherwise, it goes into a general log. Decisions are all made manually, often resulting in delays or duplicate work, and some accessibility issues remain unaddressed for days.

The Mapping Chaos
By 4:00 PM, Alex has a pile of ten incident reports. Each one requires manually checking city maps, estimating accessible routes, and planning inspection visits. This takes hours, and the printed directions often get misplaced.

The Documentation Nightmare
Citizens call back asking if their reports were logged. Alex has to dig through paper forms to find the original submission, confirm details, and record follow-ups. There’s no single reference to track repeat issues or patterns.

The Forgotten Trends
At the end of the month, management asks which areas have the highest frequency of accessibility issues. Alex spends hours manually compiling logs and realizing some critical neighborhoods were overlooked entirely.

Alex sets down his clipboard, rubs his eyes, and sighs. “There has to be a better way to track all of this—and actually keep our city accessible.”
ShortBrief: SafePath is a community-driven urban accessibility platform that connects residents with municipal services to improve city infrastructure and accessibility. Through a structured mobile form, citizens report accessibility issues using GPS, photos, severity ratings, and disability impact classifications, transforming observations into standardized, actionable data. The system aggregates submissions into an intelligent dashboard supported by automated reports and an AI-based severity index that prioritizes urgent and high-impact issues. Integrated mapping enhances accessible route planning while improving inspection and maintenance workflows. By combining community engagement with data-driven management, SafePath enables faster responses, smarter resource allocation, and more inclusive urban environments.
Mission: To help cities improve accessibility while enabling people with disabilities to safely navigate urban spaces through community-driven reporting and actionable insights.

Title: RapidMind
ExtendedDescription:
RapidMind is a digital platform designed to provide timely, effective mental health support to individuals in crisis. Many public mental health systems face significant overload, resulting in long waiting times for treatment. During acute distress, individuals often feel helpless, lacking immediate support that could prevent further deterioration or escalation. RapidMind bridges this gap by combining structured intake, volunteer coordination, AI-assisted prioritization, reporting, and geospatial services to deliver rapid and appropriate assistance.

1. Crisis Intake Module
The system begins with a structured intake interface that allows individuals in distress to request help quickly and efficiently. Users provide information such as age, gender, type of emotional or psychological issue, geographic location, a detailed description of their situation, and indicators of urgency or risk. This structured input ensures that critical information is captured consistently, forming a reliable foundation for triage and rapid response.

2. Volunteer Management Dashboard
A centralized dashboard enables coordinators and trained volunteers to access incoming requests efficiently. The dashboard supports advanced filtering and sorting by categories such as age, gender, type of distress, geographic area, description, and urgency level. Requests are matched to volunteers based on suitability; for example, adolescents may be routed to volunteers with experience in youth support, and children are matched with certified child mental health responders. This targeted approach ensures sensitive, effective handling of each case.

3. AI Prioritization Agent
The AI module continuously evaluates incoming requests to assign a dynamic urgency score. Using structured input data and natural language analysis of the descriptive text, the AI identifies high-risk cases, including severe psychological distress, potential self-harm, or exposure to violence. Urgent requests are flagged immediately and routed to qualified volunteers for prompt intervention. This AI-driven triage enhances the system’s ability to respond quickly and reliably to critical situations.

4. Reporting and Analytics Module
RapidMind includes a reporting system that generates operational and strategic insights. Reports cover key metrics such as request volume and type, response times, volunteer engagement, geographic distribution, and trends in urgency. Automated reporting allows administrators to monitor system performance, allocate resources effectively, and continuously improve service quality.

5. Geospatial Support via Cloud Mapping API
The platform integrates geospatial services to locate nearby mental health resources, such as counseling centers, hospitals, or rehabilitation facilities. Using the user’s location and mapping APIs, the system provides immediate directions or referrals to physical support services when appropriate, complementing the volunteer-based response.

Conclusion
RapidMind combines structured reporting, intelligent volunteer assignment, AI-assisted prioritization, comprehensive reporting, and geospatial awareness to create a responsive mental health support system. By providing immediate, appropriate, and targeted assistance, the platform empowers both individuals in crisis and trained volunteers to act effectively, improving mental health outcomes and reducing the risk of escalation.
